skill.name: fix-bug
skill.description: Guides an AI coding assistant through bug investigation, minimal fixes, tests, review, QA checks, and pull request preparation.
skill.summary: Fix bugs with root-cause analysis, focused code changes, regression tests, and review steps.
skill.author: Crearize

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many Markdown examples that direct the assistant to run local shell commands such as git, grep, find with xargs, Gradle, and pnpm. These commands are legitimate for a bug-fixing workflow, but they can inspect repository files, read logs, and execute project-controlled scripts, so publication should include a clear warning. The weak-cryptography, reconnaissance, and entropy alerts appear to be false positives caused by Japanese text, placeholders, documentation paths, and example code; no malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs the assistant to run local commands for branch inspection, log searches, test discovery, backend startup, and frontend startup. This is expected for bug fixing, but commands such as find with xargs, Gradle, and pnpm can read local project data or execute repository-controlled scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown command blocks and are part of the declared workflow. The risk is real because local commands and package scripts can execute code, but the context is legitimate development automation.

medium_findings.title: High-Impact Automated Development Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill directs the assistant through code changes, test generation, architecture review, QA checks, and pull request creation. This can modify repository state and prepare changes for publication, so users should review generated diffs and PR content before merging.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly includes implementation, tests, QA, and PR creation steps. It is not malicious, but the operational impact is meaningful enough to require user oversight.

low_findings.title: Broad Local Search of Code and Logs
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill asks the assistant to search backend logs, frontend files, source directories, tests, and similar bug reports. This is normal for debugging, but it may expose sensitive log content to the active AI session if users run it in repositories with secrets in logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-search and log-search instructions are explicit. I did not find evidence of network exfiltration, so the risk is limited to local disclosure within the session.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis marked weak cryptography on frontmatter, placeholders, document names, and Japanese prose. I did not find hash functions, encryption APIs, password handling, or instructions to use weak algorithms at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows metadata, placeholders, and documentation references rather than cryptographic code. The alert appears caused by keyword matching, not a real crypto implementation.

low_findings.title: Entropy and Reconnaissance Static Alerts Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-entropy alert is explained by Japanese text and structured Markdown, not binary or encrypted payloads. The system-reconnaissance alerts point to example test code and architecture-document references, with no host fingerprinting or data collection behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter delimiters, test method names, and documentation references. I found no obfuscated payload, encoded command, host enumeration, or prompt injection text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Shell Blocks Execute Local Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell examples for git, grep, find with xargs, Gradle, and pnpm. These should run only in trusted repositories because package and build commands may execute project-defined scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and executable if followed by an assistant. They are development-oriented rather than malicious, which keeps the severity at medium.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sub-Skill Invocation for Testing, QA, and PR Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow invokes other skills for backend tests, frontend tests, architecture review, QA, and pull request creation. These steps can change files and prepare publishable repository changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The invocations are explicitly listed as workflow steps. Their effect depends on the referenced skills, so the risk is operational and requires review rather than blocking.

content.user_title: Fix Bugs with Tests and Review
content.value_statement: Bug fixes can fail when the cause, regression tests, and quality checks are handled separately. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a complete fix workflow with review-ready output.
actual_capabilities: Structures bug investigation around the bug description, issue number, target area, and suspected files.
Guides source, log, and test searches to identify likely root causes.
Prompts focused backend or frontend fixes with minimal unrelated changes.
Requires regression tests for the fixed behavior and related edge cases.
Adds review, QA, server startup, and pull request preparation steps.
Produces a completion report with cause, impact, tests, and quality results.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not a standalone debugger or test runner.
It assumes the repository has backend, frontend, QA, and PR workflows matching the documented commands.
It may run local project commands that require user review in untrusted repositories.
It cannot guarantee a correct fix without accurate reproduction details and human code review.

use_cases.title: Triage Production Bugs
use_cases.description: Use a repeatable process to inspect logs, search related code, identify the root cause, and define a focused fix plan.

use_cases.title: Fix Frontend Regressions
use_cases.description: Guide component fixes, add reproduction tests, run frontend checks, and prepare a clear pull request summary.

use_cases.title: Standardize Bug-Fix Reviews
use_cases.description: Require cause analysis, minimal changes, regression tests, QA evidence, and documented review results for every bug fix.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Bug Fix
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a known issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the fix-bug skill for this issue. Bug description: [describe the bug]. Issue number: [number]. Target: [backend, frontend, or both]. Suspected files: [optional files].

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Before Editing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the fix-bug workflow to investigate this bug before making changes. First report the likely root cause, affected files, impact, and test plan. Wait for approval before editing.

prompt_templates.title: Add Regression Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Fix this bug with the smallest safe change. Add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it. Include backend or frontend QA checks that match the affected area.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Review-Ready PR
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full fix-bug process for issue #[number]. Investigate, implement, add tests, run review and QA steps, then draft a pull request summary with cause, fix, tests, risks, and verification results.

output_examples.input: A login session expires immediately after refresh.
output_examples.output: A root-cause report naming the affected session logic, a minimal fix plan, regression test coverage, QA checks, and a pull request summary.

output_examples.input: A React component logs an error after it unmounts.
output_examples.output: A focused frontend fix, a component regression test, frontend validation results, and notes about the confirmed behavior after unmount.

output_examples.input: An API returns the wrong error for missing users.
output_examples.output: A backend fix with exception handling, unit tests for missing and invalid users, review findings, and verification status.

best_practices: Run it in a trusted working tree and review every command before execution.
Provide reproduction steps, issue number, target area, and suspected files when possible.
Review generated diffs, tests, QA results, and pull request text before merging.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to make broad refactors unrelated to the reported bug.
Do not run package, build, or server commands from untrusted repositories without inspection.
Do not skip regression tests or QA evidence when the fix changes behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill fix bugs automatically?
faq.answer: It guides an AI assistant through investigation, implementation, tests, review, QA, and PR preparation. Human review is still required.

faq.question: Can it work on backend and frontend bugs?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes separate backend and frontend paths and supports a combined target when both areas are affected.

faq.question: Does it require an issue number?
faq.answer: The workflow expects an issue number for branch and PR context. You can still provide a temporary reference during investigation.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill tells the assistant to run local commands and project scripts. This is useful for debugging but needs user oversight.

faq.question: Does it send code or logs to external services?
faq.answer: No external network request is present in the skill file. It does ask the assistant to read local code and logs.

faq.question: What should I check before merging its output?
faq.answer: Review the root-cause analysis, changed files, regression tests, QA results, and pull request description.
